title: Why Caching Context Saves Money
concept: Reprocessing an identical long system prompt or document on every call wastes compute; caching reuses that work.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: When you send a prompt to an LLM, what does the model have to do with the text you send it?

options.text: Process it through the model to understand it, every single time
Nothing — it already memorized every possible prompt
Store it forever without reading it

exercises.explain: Every time you call an LLM, it has to process (read and 'think through') all the text you send — including instructions, documents, and your question — before it can respond.

cards.title: The Same Homework, Over and Over
cards.body: Imagine re-reading a 50-page manual from scratch every single time someone asks you a question about it — even if it's the exact same manual as five seconds ago. That's what LLMs do by default, and it costs real money and time.

cards.title: What Is Context Caching?
cards.body: Context caching means saving the model's already-processed version of a long, unchanging prompt (like a system prompt or document) so it doesn't have to reprocess it on every new call. The model reuses that saved work instead of redoing it.

cards.title: The Librarian Who Remembers
cards.body: Picture a librarian who has to re-read an entire textbook every time you ask a question about chapter 3 — even if you asked ten questions in a row. A smart librarian instead keeps the book open on their desk, already skimmed, ready to answer fast. Caching is that open book on the desk.

cards.title: A Customer Support Bot
cards.body: A support bot has a 3,000-word system prompt describing company policies. Without caching, every customer message forces the model to reprocess those 3,000 words first. With caching, the policies are processed once and reused for every customer that day, saving huge amounts of compute.

cards.title: Myth: Caching Makes the Model 'Remember' You
cards.body: Caching does NOT mean the model remembers your conversation like a person would. It just means the unchanged parts of your input (like a fixed instruction block) don't need to be recomputed — it's a compute shortcut, not memory or learning.

cards.title: No Cache vs. Cache
cards.body: Same long document, two very different costs when reused across many calls.

left.text: Every call reprocesses the full document from scratch — slow and expensive, again and again.

right.text: The document is processed once, then reused — faster responses and lower cost on every repeat call.

exercises.prompt: Why is reprocessing an identical long system prompt on every single API call wasteful?

options.text: Because the model forgets how to read after the first call
Because the same unchanged text gets recomputed from scratch each time, burning compute for no new benefit
Because system prompts are illegal to reuse
Because only short prompts can be processed by LLMs

exercises.explain: If the text hasn't changed, reprocessing it every time produces the exact same result as before — so all that repeated compute work is wasted effort that caching can eliminate.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Context caching means the model permanently learns and updates its knowledge from your documents.
exercises.explain: False — caching only reuses already-computed processing of unchanged text for future calls. It doesn't teach the model anything new or permanently update it.

exercises.sentence: Caching reuses previously processed work instead of reprocessing the same long context on every call.
exercises.explain: Caching's whole point is to avoid reprocessing identical content again — it reuses the work that was already done.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations of caching sounds like generic AI-written filler rather than a clear, concrete explanation?

options.text: "Caching leverages advanced synergistic optimization paradigms to holistically enhance your AI experience across all touchpoints."
"Caching saves the processed version of an unchanging prompt so the model doesn't redo that work every call."
"If your system prompt never changes, caching stores its processed form once and reuses it, cutting cost and latency."

exercises.explain: The first option is full of vague buzzwords ('synergistic optimization paradigms', 'holistically', 'touchpoints') that sound impressive but explain nothing concrete — a classic sign of AI filler text, unlike the other two which clearly describe what caching actually does.

exercises.prompt: A company's chatbot sends the same 5,000-word legal disclaimer as part of the system prompt on every single one of its 10,000 daily calls. What's the smartest fix, and why?

options.text: Delete the disclaimer entirely to save compute
Cache the processed disclaimer once and reuse it across calls, since it never changes
Ask the user to type the disclaimer themselves each time
Switch to a smaller model so processing is cheaper regardless

exercises.explain: The disclaimer is identical every time, so reprocessing all 5,000 words 10,000 times a day wastes enormous compute for zero new benefit. Caching lets the system process it once and reuse that work on every call — saving cost and speeding up responses, without losing the important legal content the way deleting it would.
